fragments.content: Cancer cachexia is a multifactorial syndrome characterized by ongoing loss of skeletal muscle mass (with or without fat loss) that cannot be fully reversed by conventional nutritional support. Diagnostic criteria: weight loss >5% over 6 months; or BMI <20 with >2% weight loss; or sarcopenia with >2% weight loss. Prevalence: 50-80% of advanced cancer patients, varies by tumor type (pancreatic 80-90%, gastric 70-80%, lung 60-70%, colorectal 50-60%). Pathophysiology: tumor-derived factors (PIF, LMF), systemic inflammation (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1, CRP), metabolic alterations (increased energy expenditure, insulin resistance, lipolysis, proteolysis via ubiquitin-proteasome pathway), anorexia (central appetite suppression), reduced physical activity. Cachexia stages: precachexia (<5% weight loss, anorexia, metabolic changes); cachexia (meets criteria); refractory cachexia (active catabolism, poor performance status, life expectancy <3 months). Impact: reduces treatment tolerance, increases toxicity, impairs quality of life, independent predictor of mortality.

Management requires multimodal approach. Nutritional intervention: early dietitian referral; oral nutritional supplements (high-protein, high-calorie); omega-3 fatty acids (EPA) may stabilize weight; enteral feeding if functional GI tract and reasonable prognosis; parenteral nutrition only if intestinal failure and anticipated survival >2-3 months. Pharmacologic: megestrol acetate (progestin) improves appetite and weight (mostly fat/fluid, not muscle); corticosteroids short-term appetite improvement (dexamethasone 4-8mg daily); cannabinoids (dronabinol) limited evidence; ghrelin agonists (anamorelin approved in Japan, investigational elsewhere); NSAIDs may reduce inflammation. Exercise: resistance training preserves muscle mass when combined with nutrition; aerobic exercise improves function. Anti-cytokine approaches: anti-IL-6, anti-TNF agents investigational. Myostatin inhibitors in development. Treatment of reversible contributors: pain, depression, nausea, constipation, mucositis. Early palliative care integration improves outcomes. Refractory cachexia: focus on comfort measures, small frequent meals, favorite foods.

Assessment tools for cachexia screening and diagnosis. Weight monitoring: serial weights at each visit; >2% loss warrants evaluation. Body composition: CT-based muscle area measurement at L3 vertebra (research/tertiary centers); DEXA scan for lean body mass; bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA) estimates fat-free mass; handgrip strength dynamometry assesses muscle function. Inflammatory markers: CRP, albumin, IL-6 correlate with cachexia severity. Appetite assessment: FAACT (Functional Assessment of Anorexia/Cachexia Therapy) questionnaire; simplified eating questionnaires. Performance status: ECOG/Karnofsky scores often decline with cachexia. Staging: determine precachexia vs cachexia vs refractory based on weight loss, symptoms, prognosis. Differential diagnosis: rule out mechanical obstruction, depression, untreated pain, medication side effects, endocrine disorders (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency). Prognostic significance: cachexia associated with reduced survival independent of stage; mGPS (modified Glasgow Prognostic Score) combining CRP and albumin predicts outcomes.
